steps.title: Neurology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Primary Care

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What should a primary-level provider consider if a child with acute weakness appears at risk of respiratory compromise?
steps.rationale: In cases like Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) or polio, where respiratory compromise is a risk, the guideline advises direct referral to tertiary care for airway support.

textChoices.text: Refer the child directly to a tertiary facility
Begin oral antibiotics and schedule a follow-up
Wait for signs of full paralysis before acting
Observe for 72 hours and reassess

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: Which of the following best indicates a need for urgent tertiary-level referral in a child with acute weakness?
steps.rationale: The guideline highlights that signs of respiratory involvement in a child with acute weakness warrant immediate referral to tertiary care for potential airway management.

textChoices.text: Mild muscle pain with normal reflexes
Headache with no weakness
Fever and rash only
Rapidly progressing weakness with signs of respiratory difficulty

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the appropriate action when a child presents with new-onset neurological signs at a primary health facility?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, any child with new neurological symptoms should be referred to a secondary-level facility for further evaluation and management.

textChoices.text: Begin full neurological investigations onsite
Start corticosteroids and observe for 48 hours
Refer to a secondary healthcare facility for initial work-up and acute care
Initiate long-term rehabilitation therapy

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is the immediate priority when a child presents with suspected stroke at a primary care facility?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes ABCDE management, neuroprotection, and urgent referral as the primary steps in stroke care at the primary level.

textChoices.text: Wait for imaging before taking action
Administer aspirin and observe for 24 hours
Begin rehabilitation exercises
Apply ABCDE approach and provide neuroprotective care, then refer urgently

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: Why is temperature control important in the management of acute pediatric stroke?
steps.rationale: The guideline notes that keeping the child cool is essential because high temperatures can exacerbate ischemic injury.

textChoices.text: High temperatures can worsen cerebral ischemia
Fever helps identify infection-related strokes
Hypothermia prevents seizures
Temperature changes are unrelated to stroke outcome

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is the recommended approach to blood sugar management in a child with acute stroke?
steps.rationale: The guideline stresses the importance of maintaining normal glycaemia as part of neuroprotective care during acute stroke management.

textChoices.text: Induce mild hypoglycemia for neuroprotection
Avoid checking glucose until after referral
Maintain normal blood glucose levels
Only treat high glucose if above 12 mmol/L

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is the appropriate action at a primary care facility when a child presents with suspected hydrocephalus?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises primary-level providers to apply ABCCCD (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Consciousness, Convulsions, Dehydration) management and refer the patient for further care.

textChoices.text: Begin corticosteroid therapy immediately
Manage using the ABCCCD approach and refer
Perform lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
Schedule follow-up in two weeks if symptoms persist

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: What is the appropriate action at a primary care level when a child presents with suspected blocked VP shunt?
steps.rationale: At the primary care level, the guideline recommends initial stabilization using ABCCCD (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Consciousness, Convulsions, Dehydration), followed by urgent referral for specialized care.

textChoices.text: Begin corticosteroids and monitor
Attempt to flush the shunt manually
Manage using the ABCCCD approach
Reposition the child and observe for 24 hours

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the recommendation regarding immunizations for children with Cerebral Palsy?
steps.rationale: Children with CP should receive all immunizations on schedule unless specific contraindications exist.

textChoices.text: Only administer vaccines once growth has stabilized
Delay all live vaccines
Use reduced doses due to muscle spasticity
Administer all routine vaccines

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: How should nutrition be addressed at the primary care level in a child with CP?
steps.rationale: Children with CP are prone to feeding difficulties and undernutrition, so ongoing assessment and support are essential.

textChoices.text: Provide multivitamins and discharge
Refer directly for feeding tube placement
Assess regularly and provide nutritional rehabilitation if undernourished
Avoid nutritional evaluation until weight is severely low

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What should be done during routine Under-5 clinic visits at primary care level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends routine developmental screening and early referral when delays are noted.

textChoices.text: Assess for hearing only
Screen for developmental delay and refer if needed
Only weigh and measure head circumference
Wait for parental concern before assessing milestones

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: Which of the following is an essential component of primary-level care for a child with Cerebral Palsy?
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes education of the child, guardians, and community as a key role of primary-level care in managing Cerebral Palsy.

textChoices.text: Surgical correction of motor symptoms
Education of the child, guardians, and the community
Long-term sedation for muscle tone control
Home-based ventilation support

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What is the recommended initial action when a child presents with an acute seizure at a primary health care facility?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises treating acute seizures using ABCDE and the seizure protocol as the first-line approach.

textChoices.text: Refer immediately without intervention
Administer long-term antiepileptics immediately
Manage using ABCDE and the seizure protocol
Perform lumbar puncture before treating the seizure

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What practical advice can be given to caregivers at the primary level to support epilepsy diagnosis?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends that caregivers record seizure episodes on video when possible, to help clinicians confirm the diagnosis.

textChoices.text: Take a video of the seizure episode if they have access to a phone
Begin documenting food triggers
Record the child’s blood pressure during each episode
Keep the child awake during episodes for observation

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: A newborn is delivered with an obvious neural tube defect. What is the most appropriate initial step in management at the primary level?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the focus is on stabilization: assessing the baby’s airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure (ABCDE), maintaining warmth, and prompt referral to a higher level of care. No definitive treatment is done at this stage.

textChoices.text: Cover the defect with antibiotic ointment and discharge home
Begin oral antibiotics immediately
Attempt surgical closure of the defect
Assess ABCDE and keep the baby warm

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is a recommended strategy when managing recurrent headaches in children?
steps.rationale: Keeping a detailed diary helps identify headache patterns and triggers, which is a foundational step in headache management.

textChoices.text: Start medication immediately without diary tracking
Keep a diary recording quality, severity, timing, and triggers
Advise complete school absence until headache-free
Avoid all physical activity for at least one week

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What advice should be given regarding school attendance for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Children should return to school, and short breaks (e.g., 15 minutes in the nurse’s office during peak headache) are acceptable to help them cope.

textChoices.text: Keep them home until headaches fully resolve
Encourage return to school with planned breaks if needed
Restrict school participation to half-days permanently
Recommend homeschooling due to stress factors

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is not a recommended lifestyle modification for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Overuse of analgesics can worsen headaches; medication overuse is a known risk factor for chronic headaches.

textChoices.text: Avoiding headache triggers like caffeine and poor sleep
Daily physical exercise
Using painkillers daily to prevent headache onset
Managing comorbid sleep problems

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What is an important goal of patient education in headache management?
steps.rationale: Empowering patients enhances self-control and can significantly reduce distress and improve outcomes in chronic headache management.

textChoices.text: Minimize the use of diaries and self-monitoring
Focus solely on pharmacological solutions
Enable patients to manage their disease and reduce psychological distress
Encourage complete avoidance of social interaction
